message:
fix(unistd): hoist EFAULT guard ahead of bootstrap-passthrough

Moves the tracked-buffer EFAULT check in read() and write() above the
bootstrap_passthrough_active fast path so an oversized tracked buffer
is rejected even during early-startup passthrough. Previously the
guard sat after the bootstrap branch — when membrane decisions are
suppressed during init, the read/write would hand the bogus pointer
straight to the kernel, producing whatever the syscall surfaced
(usually EFAULT, sometimes a partial transfer with stale-tail bytes
written) instead of the deterministic EFAULT-without-syscall the
guard exists to provide.

The new placement also drops the now-redundant runtime_policy::
observe() call from the EFAULT path. observe() existed there to
record the rejection in policy meters, but during bootstrap there
is no policy mode to record against, and once the policy is up the
observe call's previous position guaranteed `decision` was already
computed — pre-policy callers had no `decision.profile` to pass.
The simpler form just sets errno and returns -1; rejected
transfers count toward syscall-failure metrics through the
unchanged kernel-layer accounting.

Refactor only — the post-policy check used to fire AFTER decide()
ran, which means a rejected transfer used to incur a policy
decision and then bail. Now the policy decision is skipped for
the same rejection; the user-visible errno is unchanged.
